stories.headline: US-Iran War Enters Third Week as Trump Calls for International Support at Strait of Hormuz
stories.summary: The military conflict between the US, Israel, and Iran has entered its third week, with President Trump urging allied nations to help secure the Strait of Hormuz after Iranian forces disrupted critical oil shipping lanes. Iran's foreign minister publicly disputed Trump's claims that Tehran seeks negotiations, escalating diplomatic tensions. The Defense Department released the names of six US service members killed in a fuel tanker crash in western Iraq.

stories.headline: Oil Spike to $119 Drives Market Rotation Into Defensive Sectors as Energy Stocks Surge
stories.summary: WTI crude surged to $119 per barrel before retreating below $100, driven by Strait of Hormuz disruptions from the Iran conflict. Energy became the top-performing S&P 500 sector year-to-date as investors rotated into defensive consumer staples and utilities. The IEA released 400 million barrels from strategic reserves to ease global supply pressures.

stories.headline: Fed Expected to Hold Rates as Iran War Compounds Inflation Pressures
stories.summary: Major central banks including the US Federal Reserve are expected to hold interest rates steady this week as the Iran war's energy shock adds to persistent inflation concerns. Bond yields rose and markets priced out rate cuts until late 2026, with inflation breakevens exceeding 2.6%. The combination of tariff-driven price increases and oil supply disruptions has left the Fed with limited policy flexibility.

stories.headline: FCC Chair Threatens Broadcast License Reviews Over War Coverage
stories.summary: FCC Chair Brendan Carr accused broadcasters of running 'hoaxes and news distortions' in their coverage of the Iran war and threatened to review broadcast TV licenses at renewal. The FCC's sole Democratic commissioner Ana Gomez responded that such threats violate the First Amendment. The move raises concerns about press freedom during wartime and follows a pattern of administration pressure on media outlets.

stories.headline: Oracle Surges 9% on AI Cloud Strength, Raises FY2027 Guidance
stories.summary: Oracle shares jumped 9% after beating earnings expectations on resilient AI cloud demand, raising its FY2027 guidance without additional debt issuance. The results fueled broader AI investment optimism even as markets contended with Iran-driven volatility. Amazon simultaneously issued €14.5 billion in bonds to fund AI infrastructure expansion.

stories.headline: Fertilizer Stocks Rally 10-12% on Iran War Supply Shortages
stories.summary: Shares of Nutrien, CF Industries, and Mosaic surged 10-12% as the Iran conflict created fertilizer supply shortages, compounding inflationary pressures alongside the oil shock. The rally reflects growing concerns about food price inflation cascading from energy disruptions. Agricultural input costs are expected to rise further if the conflict extends into planting season.

stories.headline: Oracle Beats Earnings on AI Cloud Demand, Amazon Issues €14.5B for AI Infrastructure
stories.summary: Oracle's 9% stock surge on strong AI cloud results highlights sustained enterprise AI adoption despite broader market turbulence from the Iran conflict. Amazon's simultaneous €14.5 billion bond issuance for AI infrastructure signals that hyperscaler investment in compute capacity remains aggressive. Both moves underscore that AI spending is becoming non-discretionary for major tech players.

stories.headline: AI Infrastructure Investment Dominates VC as Compute Distribution Becomes Critical
stories.summary: Capital is rapidly shifting toward AI infrastructure companies providing computing power, chips, energy, and data center capacity rather than foundation models. Major deals including Advanced Machine Intelligence's $1 billion raise, Nscale's $2 billion funding, and Thinking Machines Lab's Nvidia partnership signal that distributing compute has become as strategic as the capital itself.

stories.headline: Domain-Specific AI Models Replace Generalist Approaches Across Industries
stories.summary: The 2026 AI landscape is shifting decisively toward specialized, domain-specific models tailored for healthcare, legal, financial, and industrial applications rather than giant generalist systems. This transition is driven by enterprises discovering that smaller, fine-tuned models deliver superior performance on narrow tasks at a fraction of the compute cost. The trend accelerates as companies seek ROI from AI deployments beyond experimentation.

stories.headline: Model Context Protocol Emerges as Standard for AI Agent Interoperability
stories.summary: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is gaining rapid adoption as the de facto standard for enabling AI agents to interact with external tools, databases, and services. Major AI companies are integrating MCP support to allow workflows that chain multiple AI systems together seamlessly. The protocol addresses a key bottleneck in enterprise AI deployment: connecting models to real-world data and actions.

stories.headline: Physical AI Advances in Robotics and Autonomous Vehicles Accelerate
stories.summary: Physical AI applications in robotics and autonomous vehicles are entering a new phase of deployment, with companies leveraging improved foundation models for real-world manipulation and navigation. Nvidia's partnerships and compute infrastructure are enabling more sophisticated embodied AI systems. The convergence of better models, cheaper sensors, and improved simulation is reducing the gap between lab demos and production deployments.

stories.headline: EU AI Act Compliance Deadlines Drive Enterprise AI Governance Spending
stories.summary: With EU AI Act compliance deadlines taking effect in 2026, enterprises are accelerating spending on AI governance frameworks, bias auditing, and documentation systems. The NIST AI Risk Management Framework is being adopted alongside EU requirements as a dual-compliance approach. Companies face significant costs to classify AI systems by risk level and implement required transparency measures.

stories.headline: A16z Raises Record $15 Billion Across Multiple Funds Including Defense-Focused American Dynamism
stories.summary: Andreessen Horowitz announced its largest fundraising haul, raising $15 billion across funds including $6.75 billion for growth, $1.7 billion for infrastructure, and $1.176 billion for its American Dynamism practice focused on defense and security. The record raise follows North American startups pulling in $280 billion in 2025, up 46% year-over-year. The majority of capital continues to flow toward AI-related sectors.

stories.headline: Global VC Hits Record $189B in February Led by OpenAI, Anthropic, and Waymo Mega-Rounds
stories.summary: Global venture funding reached a record $189 billion in February 2026, with OpenAI ($110B), Anthropic ($30B), and Waymo ($16B) accounting for 83% of total capital invested. The extreme concentration reflects a winner-take-most dynamic in AI funding where the largest players absorb the vast majority of available capital. Remaining startups competed for roughly $32 billion across all other sectors.

stories.headline: Ayar Labs Raises $500M Series E at $3.75B Valuation for Optical Interconnect Technology
stories.summary: Ayar Labs closed a $500 million Series E round valuing the company at $3.75 billion for its optical interconnect technology that dramatically increases data center bandwidth while reducing power consumption. The funding reflects investor conviction that optical I/O is essential for scaling AI compute beyond current electrical interconnect limitations. The deal was one of several $500M+ rounds in the first week of March.

stories.headline: Vast Secures $500M for Next-Generation Commercial Space Station Development
stories.summary: Space startup Vast raised $500 million to advance development of its commercial space station, positioning itself as a leading candidate to replace the aging International Space Station. The funding round signals continued investor appetite for space infrastructure despite broader market volatility from the Iran conflict. The company aims to launch its first module by 2028.

stories.headline: Science Corp Closes $230M Series C for Brain-Computer Interface Technology
stories.summary: Alameda-based Science Corp raised $230 million in Series C funding for its brain-computer interface technology, joining a growing wave of neurotech companies attracting significant venture capital. The round underscores broadening biotech VC activity beyond pure AI plays. Findhelp also secured $250 million from TPG's Rise Fund for care coordination.

stories.headline: VC Market Restructures Around Deep Tech as Valuations Shift to Integration Depth
stories.summary: The venture investment landscape is consolidating around deep tech categories where investors see dominance potential, with valuations increasingly justified by product integration depth rather than user metrics. This structural shift favors companies solving critical infrastructure bottlenecks in compute, energy, and enterprise software. The trend marks a departure from the growth-at-all-costs era toward sustainable competitive moats.

stories.headline: UN Climate Chief Says Energy Price Hikes From Iran War Accelerate Renewable Transition
stories.summary: UN climate chief Simon Stiell told the 2026 Green Growth Summit that surging oil and gas prices from the Iran conflict are accelerating the economic case for renewables, echoing the Ukraine war's impact on European energy policy. He emphasized that solar and wind avoid vulnerabilities like shipping straits and naval escorts that plague fossil fuel supply chains. The comments frame the current crisis as a tipping point for energy independence through clean sources.

stories.headline: US Energy Prices Set to Rise From Canceled Renewables, Deregulation, and Iran War Combined
stories.summary: A Chatham House analysis warns that US consumers face rising energy costs from a triple squeeze: Iran war-driven oil spikes, canceled renewable projects, and phased-out clean energy tax incentives under the 'One Big Beautiful Bill Act.' Deregulation that removes efficiency standards and subsidizes coal could add $165-$280 annually to household bills by 2030-2035. The combination threatens to reverse recent progress on energy affordability despite strong public support for solar and wind.

stories.headline: Singapore and Japan Sign Bilateral Framework on Hydrogen, Nuclear, and Cross-Border Energy
stories.summary: Singapore and Japan launched a comprehensive energy cooperation framework covering cross-border electricity trading, low-carbon hydrogen and ammonia, carbon capture, civil nuclear energy, and offshore wind development. The agreement includes policy exchanges, business matching, and standards harmonization to accelerate decarbonization in both countries. It signals deepening Asia-Pacific energy partnerships as the region navigates both climate goals and energy security concerns.

stories.headline: Greenpeace Fights $345 Million Verdict in Dakota Access Pipeline Case
stories.summary: A North Dakota court upheld a $345 million judgment against Greenpeace for its role in Dakota Access Pipeline protests, brought by Energy Transfer claiming the organization hindered construction. Greenpeace says the sum could bankrupt its $40 million annual operations and plans appeals on free expression grounds. The case originated from non-violent protests led primarily by Native American groups a decade ago.

stories.headline: Ukraine Adds 3GW of Renewables Despite Russia Destroying Half Its Energy Infrastructure
stories.summary: Despite Russia devastating over half of Ukraine's energy infrastructure with damages exceeding $56 billion, the country has added over three gigawatts of new renewable capacity since the invasion. Grid operators plan to nearly double renewable production within four years, demonstrating clean energy's resilience in conflict zones. The achievement challenges assumptions about renewable vulnerability and provides a case study for distributed energy security.

stories.headline: North Carolina Holds Hearings on Duke Energy Carbon Plan Criticized for Gas Reliance
stories.summary: Public hearings from March 16-18 are reviewing Duke Energy's 2026 Carbon Plan, which doubles down on methane gas plants and cancels offshore wind commitments amid federal policy shifts. Critics argue the plan fails to chart a clear path to net-zero by 2050 and keeps coal plants operating longer than necessary. Adjusted modeling that includes the 2050 target shows a stronger case for clean energy over gas.

stories.headline: US Withdraws from IRENA as Europe Defends Renewable Energy Commitments
stories.summary: The US announced withdrawal from the International Renewable Energy Agency under Trump administration policies prioritizing fossil fuels, slashing its funding contribution. European governments have publicly defended their renewable energy transitions in response, highlighting a growing transatlantic divide on climate policy. The move aligns with broader US pullbacks from international climate agreements and institutions.

stories.headline: FDA Approves Leucovorin for Cerebral Folate Deficiency, Not Autism Despite Administration Push
stories.summary: The FDA approved leucovorin, a synthetic vitamin B9, for treating cerebral folate deficiency — a rare neurological condition — despite Trump administration promotion of its use for autism symptoms. The approval does not extend to autism treatment, creating a gap between regulatory reality and political messaging. The distinction highlights tensions between evidence-based drug approval and political pressure on health agencies.

stories.headline: Medicare Part B Premiums Rose 10% Due to Overpayments to Medicare Advantage Plans
stories.summary: Congressional investigators found that average Medicare Part B premiums increased roughly 10% last year, adding over $200 annually per senior, driven by overpayments to private Medicare Advantage plans from upcoding practices. Insurers have been adding diagnoses to patient records to trigger higher reimbursements from the government. The finding intensifies the debate over Medicare Advantage's cost-effectiveness compared to traditional Medicare.

stories.headline: MedPAC Recommends 2027 Medicare Payment Updates Including Safety-Net Hospital Boost
stories.summary: The Medicare Payment Advisory Commission released its March 2026 report recommending current-law payment amounts for acute care hospitals and above-current-law increases for physician services. It also proposes $1 billion in additional funding for safety-net hospitals through a new index and recommends reductions for skilled nursing, home health, and inpatient rehab facilities. The recommendations aim to address growing financial strain on providers serving vulnerable populations.

stories.headline: CMS Strengthens State Oversight of Medicaid Managed Care Across 14 Areas
stories.summary: CMS released guidance requiring states to enhance monitoring of managed care organizations in Medicaid and CHIP programs across 14 areas including claims processing, network adequacy, and quality improvement. The move responds to growing concerns about accountability gaps in privatized Medicaid administration. States must now demonstrate more rigorous oversight to maintain federal funding.

stories.headline: Florida Senate Passes Measure to Preserve AIDS Drug Program Funding
stories.summary: The Florida Senate approved legislation to prevent cuts to a program that supplies medications to AIDS patients, though House approval remains uncertain. The measure addresses concerns about state-level erosion of HIV/AIDS treatment access amid broader federal health spending debates. Florida has one of the nation's largest populations of people living with HIV.

stories.headline: Northwestern Memorial Hospital Approved for $96 Million ICU Expansion
stories.summary: A state board approved Northwestern Memorial Hospital's $96 million project to add intensive care unit beds as part of its broader growth strategy in Chicago. The expansion reflects ongoing demand for critical care capacity that accelerated during and after the COVID pandemic. Hospital systems continue investing in ICU infrastructure as patient acuity levels remain elevated.

stories.headline: ARPA-H Launches $144 Million PROSPR Program for Large-Scale Aging Intervention Trials
stories.summary: The US ARPA-H agency announced $144 million in funding for the PROSPR program, which aims to conduct the first large clinical studies of interventions to slow biological aging and extend healthspan. The program will use surrogate biomarkers like epigenetic clocks and walking speed to accelerate trial timelines. Experts predict this will validate aging biomarkers for faster regulatory approval of anti-aging therapies.

stories.headline: Life Biosciences Wins FDA Approval for First Epigenetic Reprogramming Trial in Humans
stories.summary: Life Biosciences, co-founded by Harvard's David Sinclair, received FDA clearance for a Phase 1 trial testing partial epigenetic reprogramming gene therapy to restore vision in glaucoma and NAION patients. The approach uses three Yamanaka factors to rejuvenate optic nerve cells without tumor risk, marking a staged path toward broader de-aging therapies. This represents the first FDA-approved human trial of cellular age reversal technology.

stories.headline: Longitudinal Epigenetic Clock Acceleration Predicts Mortality Better Than Baseline Age
stories.summary: New analysis from the InCHIANTI cohort demonstrates that the rate of epigenetic clock acceleration — how fast biological age increases over time — independently predicts mortality risk better than static epigenetic age measurements. The finding emphasizes the importance of tracking aging trajectories rather than single timepoint measurements. This could enable earlier clinical intervention for individuals whose biological aging is accelerating.

stories.headline: GESDA Radar Reports Longevity Science Crossing Clinical Threshold
stories.summary: The 2026 GESDA Science Breakthrough Radar reports that longevity research has shifted from theoretical to clinical reality, driven by advances in epigenetic clocks, molecular diagnostics, and drugs that demonstrably slow biological aging. Swiss EPFL's work on mitochondrial pathways and new centers like CERL are accelerating development of verifiable biological age metrics. The report suggests longevity interventions could enter mainstream medicine within the decade.

stories.headline: Buck Institute CEO Highlights CD38 Inhibitors and Glucosamine as Promising Aging Modulators
stories.summary: Buck Institute CEO Eric Verdin presented data on small-molecule CD38 inhibitors that correct immune aging and extend mouse lifespan, alongside unexpected longevity benefits from glucosamine via metabolic pathways. These interventions represent potentially affordable, scalable approaches to slowing aging compared to gene therapies. Both compounds will be tested alongside intrinsic capacity markers in upcoming human trials.

stories.headline: TMRW Bio Raises Funding for Actionable Biological Age Testing Platform
stories.summary: TMRW Bio secured funding for an advanced biological age measurement platform that integrates lifestyle and environmental data with ongoing clinical interpretation, moving beyond passive biomarker reporting. The platform provides personalized intervention recommendations based on individual aging trajectories. The company represents a growing category of longevity diagnostics startups translating research breakthroughs into consumer and clinical products.

stories.headline: NASA Approves Artemis 2 Rollout for April 1 Crewed Moon Mission Launch
stories.summary: NASA completed its flight readiness review and approved rollout of the SLS rocket to the launchpad by March 19, targeting an April 1 launch for Artemis 2 — the first crewed lunar mission since Apollo 17 in 1972. Four astronauts will fly a nine-day lunar flyby test validating the Orion spacecraft's life support and navigation systems. The mission is a critical stepping stone toward Artemis 3's planned lunar landing.

stories.headline: SpaceX Hits 25 Starlink Missions in 2026 With Record 32nd Booster Flight
stories.summary: SpaceX launched 25 Starlink satellites from Vandenberg using Falcon 9 booster B1071 on its record 32nd flight, marking the company's 25th Starlink mission of 2026. The booster successfully landed on the drone ship 'Of Course I Still Love You,' demonstrating continued improvements in reusability economics. SpaceX has already deployed over 600 Starlink satellites this year alone.

stories.headline: Vast Raises $500M to Build Commercial Replacement for International Space Station
stories.summary: Space startup Vast secured $500 million in funding to develop a commercial space station intended to eventually replace the aging ISS. The company aims to launch its first module by 2028, positioning itself alongside Axiom Space in the commercial station race. The funding round was one of several $500M+ space deals in early March, signaling strong investor confidence in space infrastructure.

stories.headline: SpaceX Launches Back-to-Back Starlink Missions From Both Coasts in Single Day
stories.summary: SpaceX conducted dual Falcon 9 launches on March 1 from both Vandenberg and Cape Canaveral, deploying 54 Starlink satellites total in a single day. Both boosters — B1078 and B1082 — recovered successfully on their respective drone ships. The operational tempo demonstrates SpaceX's ability to sustain near-daily launch cadence across multiple launch sites.

stories.headline: SpaceX Schedules Starlink Group 17-24 Launch from Vandenberg for March 17
stories.summary: SpaceX has scheduled another Starlink launch from Vandenberg Space Force Base for March 17 at 2:37 AM UTC, continuing its aggressive constellation expansion pace. The mission will add to the over 7,000 Starlink satellites already in orbit providing global broadband coverage. The rapid cadence is driven by increasing demand for satellite internet services worldwide.

stories.headline: Blue Origin New Glenn Debut Slips Past February Target Into Spring 2026
stories.summary: Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket has delayed its inaugural launch past the late February target, with preparations now extending into spring 2026. The heavy-lift vehicle is designed to compete with SpaceX's Falcon Heavy for national security and commercial launch contracts. Continued delays risk ceding further market share to SpaceX's increasingly dominant launch operations.

stories.headline: NFL Eyes Early Renegotiation of $110B TV Deals With Potential 50% Revenue Increase
stories.summary: The NFL plans to renegotiate its massive domestic broadcast agreements with Amazon, CBS, ESPN, Fox, and NBC before 2026, leveraging an opt-out clause and David Ellison's Paramount acquisition to demand higher values. Executives anticipate a potential 50% revenue increase that could push total deal value well above the current $110 billion. Some games may shift to streamers like Netflix or YouTube TV, raising concerns about local ad inventory erosion.

stories.headline: Netflix Acquires Warner Bros. Discovery for $82.7B in Largest Streaming Deal Ever
stories.summary: Netflix's blockbuster $82.7 billion acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery represents the largest streaming deal in history, dramatically reshaping the media landscape. The deal intensifies consolidation alongside the Paramount-Skydance transaction and boosts sports IP values as seen in the recent $10 billion Lakers sale. Capital is flowing aggressively into team ownership, stadiums, and media rights as sports becomes an institutional asset class.

stories.headline: Disney Locks in $1.3B Exclusive College Football Playoff Rights Through 2032
stories.summary: Disney's ABC and ESPN secured exclusive rights to the full College Football Playoff in a six-year, $1.3 billion deal starting 2026, while Warner Bros. Discovery sublicenses up to four games annually through TNT until 2028. The agreement underscores the escalating value of college football content amid a fragmented media landscape. CFP rights have become among the most sought-after properties in sports media.

stories.headline: Netflix Secures MLB Home Run Derby and Opening Night in $35M Three-Year Deal
stories.summary: Netflix signed a three-year, $35 million deal with MLB for exclusive coverage of the Home Run Derby, Opening Night game, and select regular season events from 2026-2028. The deal expands Netflix's growing sports portfolio following its NFL Christmas Day games. It positions the streamer as an increasingly serious player in live sports rights alongside traditional broadcasters.

stories.headline: NASCAR Unveils $1.1B/Year Media Deals With Warner, Amazon, and Nexstar
stories.summary: NASCAR finalized seven-year agreements totaling $1.1 billion annually, including Warner Bros. Discovery for five Cup Series events, Amazon Prime Video for five events, and Nexstar's CW Network for $114 million Xfinity Series rights through 2031. The deals highlight streaming's growing role in motorsports and represent a massive increase over NASCAR's previous media arrangements. Amazon's involvement marks another step in the tech giant's live sports strategy.

stories.headline: PGA of America Extends NBC and USA Sports Media Deal Through 2033
stories.summary: The PGA of America extended its media rights deal with NBC Sports and USA Sports by two years through 2033, covering the Ryder Cup, KPMG Women's PGA Championship, and other major events. The extension continues a 44-year relationship and aligns with NBC's aggressive sports rights spending exceeding $8 billion annually. Golf media rights have surged alongside the broader boom in live sports content valuation.
